meta.title: The Case for Permanent Capital Vehicles

transcript:
Thank you. Hello everyone. Um, I was just informed that this is actually the longest talk at Breakpoint this year, and so I decided to make it the most boring 20 minutes of your day and talk to you about permanent capital. Um, but hopefully we put enough memes and hot takes in here to keep you entertained a little bit. Um, and to start with, LPs make you dumb.

So, I am Joe Eagan, the co-founder of Anagram. We are a permanent capital crypto holding company. We build products in the crypto ecosystem, we incubate products, and we also invest directly off our balance sheet into crypto projects, uh, portfolio companies, and, uh, tokens.

Um, when we started Anagram, we wanted to think natively and from first principles about how to allocate capital in the crypto space. Um, and we'll get more into why I think permanent capital is so important, particularly in the crypto ecosystem, but really all across the board.

Before we do that though, it might take, might be worth a few minutes on just capital in general. Um, so capital I think of in three broad buckets. There is, uh, financial capital, human capital, and physical capital. We take these resources and we deploy them and hope to create some amount of value. Historically and in traditional finance, we have done this in silos and then generate hopefully some sort of financial return off of that capital.

But as we think about investing this capital, LPs have done a number of different things to actually make the allocation of that capital, in my view, dumber. There's really four things that I think go into this. There's the investment program and the limitations that are put upon that. There's the time or term of investment that LPs put upon any of the funds that they invest in. There's the, uh, concept of return on investment relative to fees generated on those investments. And then there's also capital allocation and how we think about actually allocating that capital across each of those three different buckets I just mentioned.

To start with, let's talk about investment program or how funds think about building out their alpha strategy or what they can actually invest in. If you think about a fund, there's really three overlapping constituents that need to be considered. First and foremost, your expertise or the CIO's expertise. What is the market opportunity then that they want to extract with that expertise? So maybe I'm a crypto portfolio management and a manager in a liquid fund and I'm going to invest into the crypto market, um, where I see prices to be down. Um, at the same time, there's investor demand. What do investors actually want? And then you have that overlap. In this, uh, picture here you can see it's actually quite small. As you add more constituents to this, that, uh, surface area actually reduces quite significantly. And so it reduces your ability as the investment manager to deploy capital fully across your area of expertise.

And so if you eliminate the LPs from this model, you have a far bigger area of alpha to generate in the market. What's interesting about this is not only is the LP limiting your ability to allocate capital according to your area of expertise, but they are also limiting it to the area that they know best. Typically LPs like to invest in things that they know and understand, which is somewhat ironic given that they're also generally paying you, the portfolio manager, fees for allocating capital in that space.

And since they don't understand usually the most complex part of your strategy, they're going to limit you to the part that they best know and understand, thereby limiting you from the most alpha-driven part of your actual strategy. And so by eliminating that LP, you're growing it, but you're also growing it to the area that least people know and understand and therefore where you can generate the most alpha.

So in addition to the investment program expansion that you get by eliminating the LP from your model, you also have the, uh, uh, the impact of time. Right, so if you think about a traditional venture fund, that's a 10-year vehicle. Um, that can either expand your time, maybe you hold an asset too long, or compress your time. Maybe you don't invest in certain things because you think it will be outside of that investment program.

But even more interestingly, if you think about liquid funds or hedge funds, you have funds that are, uh, open to any sort of redemption windows. Let's call it a month or three months. So now when an LP wants their capital back, you are a forced seller of assets. And one great example of this would be, uh, let's say, uh, FTX collapses, uh, for example, and everyone gets scared 'cause they don't know what's actually happening in Solana the ecosystem and Solana the asset. You now have a bunch of forced selling at the worst possible time and, uh, at a time when most investors should actually be allocating more capital to this space. That would have been your best opportunity for investment in the liquid markets in crypto over the past five years. And so by having LPs that are capable of taking capital back when they want it, as opposed to when you think it should be returned to them, you are also limiting your ability to make excess returns in those moments of pure opportunity.

On to return on investment relative to fees. I think one thing that is interesting about funds is that because of the fee structure, typically something like 2 and 20, there is an incentivization for asset growth over returns. The dynamic actually, uh, exists as carry is charged on absolute dollars raised as opposed to absolute return generated on a percentage basis.

So to best highlight how this actually works to, I think, the detriment of underlying investors, here's a quick example. In Fund A, you've got $100 million with a 100% return, therefore $100 million, and a carry of $20 million. The fees on management are $2 million. And then we relate that to Fund B, a far smaller fund with a far higher return. This fund returns 10,000%, also generating $100 million and a $20 million carry. This $20,000 management fee is obviously substantially less as well. So here we have a business model where the portfolio manager is actually incentivized to have higher assets and a lower return profile relative to a higher ROI where you actually make less money.

And then finally, capital allocation. Capital allocation has typically been bucketed into these two buckets. Financial capital is allocated by funds. Corporate capital is allocated to humans to then go and build things. This has been fine in the world of Web2 where typically these capital barriers have been siloed. Funds have invested in things, uh, corporations have built things.

Um, but going forward, I think it really fails to, uh, be successful in the crypto markets where building and investing come hand in hand. And that gets us to crypto itself. Crypto is really the first unique asset class in the past, I don't even know, probably since equities. And so we have this very unique asset class. It is interoperable and composable. It is open source. Um, you can build things on top of other things and you can do this at your leisure, um, in a totally permissionless environment.

And yet we have taken the same exact business models for allocating capital in the crypto space that we have always done in the past. We have funds for financial capital and corporations for, uh, human capital. And instead, I think we should be doing this in a more entangled way.

So in addition to, uh, each of the buckets that we just described—investment program, time, ROI and fees, and capital allocation—each of these buckets gets more exaggerated in the crypto space. In the crypto space, if you think about the investment program, fewer people really know anything about it, right? It is more technical, it's a low-level technology. Um, and so it is even harder to understand for those LPs and they constrict that investment program even more.

Similarly, if you think about time on a time scale, there's a tokenization, investors see liquidity, they want that capital back as quickly as possible, thereby incentivizing you to be a seller of that asset as opposed to a long-term benefactor of the ecosystem and a holder of that asset.

Similarly on ROI, as funds have grown, so too have the valuations at which people are willing to invest in, thereby reducing the long-term ROI to an even greater degree in crypto, I believe, than in other spaces.

And then finally capital allocation, where this really comes to a head. We are still today limiting the ability for investing in and building in the crypto ecosystem in one coordinated effort.

So maybe just to give a few quick examples of where I've seen this come to bear on the market. Um, in 2017, when we were allocating capital into the liquid crypto markets, most investors told us that they would not invest with us because crypto, uh, did not have custody solutions. And in fact, that was true. We were using Ledgers and other internal, uh, methodologies. But because we custodied our own assets, LPs didn't invest with us and missed what I believe to be probably the greatest investment returns of our lifetime.

Similarly, if we think about things like monthly returns, uh, or quarterly returns and the reporting of those returns, and then lay on top of that the volatility of a crypto asset, as investors see those returns, managers begin to manage to those returns. And instead of going for it and, you know, buying more of the asset at the right time, either they become the forced seller we just discussed, or they end up managing to the return instead of the long-term value of the asset and the long-term value of the ecosystem.

Where this really flips on its head though is in the inability for the capital allocation to work together, where humans can build things and also invest in things, uh, side by side. So if you think about the crypto ecosystem, if you have built Solana, you should also invest in Drift or Jupiter or any of the millions of projects that are here that all warrant investment. At the same time, if you have built a lending protocol on Solana, it also would make sense to then buy as a founder Solana and put it into a lending pool, or to invest in a tranching mechanism that would sit on top of that lending protocol. In this way, the more you invest into the space, the more value you create for the things that you have built. And the more that you build in the space, the investments that you make can interoperate with the underlying assets on your balance sheet.

To maybe put this into more specific examples about how we think about this at Anagram. We have Anagram Staking Services. We launched Anagram Staking Services as a validator for a balance sheet position that we have in Solana. We had a big enough Solana position that we wanted to validate ourselves as opposed to paying out external commissions.

As we began running this, we also decided more recently to invest and lead the investment of a pre-seed round for Rakurai, who's also here today and I encourage everyone to reach out. Rakurai is an efficient scheduler, um, who, uh, uh, came to market, um, and we became their first customer. We, uh, use Rakurai today in Anagram Staking Services and we have on any given day typically the most performant validator in the Solana ecosystem. I welcome you all by the way to delegate, uh, to our validator. Uh, reach out if you have any questions, but stake.anagram.xyz, shameless plug.

Um, we do have the most performant validator and also one of the largest validators in the network. In this way, our validator has benefited Rakurai. We became their first customer. Rakurai has benefited us. We now run the highest performing validator in the network. And all of that then also benefits Solana the ecosystem and Solana the asset. Solana the asset will hopefully go up in price as blockspace becomes more efficiently packed. And Solana the ecosystem has all of the benefits of not just our validator and Rakurai, but more efficient blockspace as well.

There's many such examples where we have, uh, done this on our balance sheet. There's also a few other projects that are speaking here today and tomorrow. Um, we, uh, spun out a project called Glider. Glider is an efficient portfolio management tool where it has fully abstracted all chains so that you can create portfolios, um, trading, lending, whatever you want using AI tools in a very sleek and unique UI.

Um, Glider was not yet ready to incorporate Solana because they did not have the appropriate tooling and wallet infrastructure to do so. After realizing that this issue was, um, uh, abundant with Glider, we decided to then build Swig, wallet infrastructure that is now its own company as well, where there's things like roles, permissions, paymaster, all for use in the Solana ecosystem. And Glider is now incorporating that technology and able to fully abstract Solana, Solana's chain, into its technology.

So every time that we make an investment or build a company at Anagram, we want to solve for three things. We want that project to be valuable itself. In this example, we want Anagram Staking Services to be a valuable enterprise in and of itself. We also want it to benefit our portfolio. In this case, Rakurai. And in Rakurai's case, we want Rakurai, something we have built and invested, to also be valuable to our balance sheet. And at the same time, we want to be valuable back to the ecosystem and be owners and holders of those underlying ecosystems. In this case, Solana. By running a validator, by running the most performant validator, and by, uh, working on these two things together, we think we not just benefit Solana the ecosystem, but selfishly Solana the asset price that we continue to hold on our balance sheet.

So if we think about permanent capital versus funds, we think that by having a permanent capital vehicle where we sold equity in our business as opposed to raised capital from LPs, we have a model where we can do what we want with that capital when we want and for whatever term we want. This allows us to continue to make the investments that we want. We can make venture bets, which we do. We can make liquid bets, which we do. We can have an EIR program where we have entrepreneurs come into our house and work with us for 12 months. We help them think through the idea maze, what kind of company they might want to build. We also have a build program where we are consistently hiring engineers to come and build new projects like the validator, like Glider, like Swig, like Breeze, like so many other projects that are soon to launch after that.

We have this, um, enormous arsenal of skills and tools in-house where we can now combine all of these assets together that all mutually benefit one another for compounding value not just for ourselves but for the ecosystem. And so anyone out there today or anyone out there on the internet that's watching, we welcome anyone to please reach out to Anagram. We have permanent capital to deploy to you, whether you are a founder, an engineer, a CEO, whoever you might be in the ecosystem. We're always looking for more partners anywhere in the world and look forward to speaking to all of you very soon. Thanks very much and LPs make you dumb.

Dec 3, 2025 11:47 AM Eastern Standard Time
# **Franklin Templeton Expands Its Digital Asset ETP Suite With Launch of Franklin Solana ETF (SOEZ)**
Share
*SOEZ provides institutional access to one of the fastest growing blockchain networks through a regulated, transparent ETF structure*
SAN MATEO, Calif.--([BUSINESS WIRE](https://www.businesswire.com))--Franklin Templeton, a global investment leader with more than $1.6 trillion in assets under management, today announced the launch of Franklin Solana ETF (NYSE Arca: SOEZ) (the “Fund”), an exchange traded product (ETP) designed to provide investors with exposure to the price movements of Solana (SOL), the native token of one of the most active and scalable blockchain networks. SOEZ seeks to reflect generally the performance of the price of Solana and the rewards from staking as much of the Fund’s Solana as is practicable (up to 100%), before payment of the Fund’s expenses and liabilities. The Fund receives staking rewards in the form of Solana tokens (SOL), which may be treated as income. Staking Solana on the network may also enhance network security and stability. The Fund’s SOL cash value is calculated by valuing the SOL held by the Fund based on the CME CF Solana-Dollar Reference Rate, New York

tags.impact_reason: The keynote discusses permanent capital vehicles and their importance in crypto, which is relevant to institutional investment strategies within the Solana ecosystem.

visual_idea.reasoning: I chose an interwoven crystalline lattice as the Semantic Anchor to represent Anagram's concept of 'permanent' and 'entangled' capital. It visually contrasts the fluid, strong, and interconnected new model against the rigid, crumbling concrete 'silos' of the old GP/LP structure. For the homepage, I integrated the Greek letters for Financial (Φ), Human (Ψ), and Physical (Ρ) capital, subtly etched onto the old silos. This is a diegetic way to represent the fragmented components that Anagram's model unifies, directly referencing the transcript's 'three broad buckets' of capital.
visual_idea.description: A macro, close-up view focusing on a central node within the crystalline capital structure. Dozens of glowing, internally-lit filaments seamlessly intersect with geometric precision. The light pulses softly, revealing intricate patterns within the transparent material. The background is a soft, out-of-focus bokeh of other glowing intersections within the vast, complex lattice, creating a sense of infinite, ordered complexity.

final_prompt.prompt_text: A macro, close-up view focusing on a central node within the crystalline capital structure, sculpted with brutalist geometric precision. Dozens of glowing, internally-lit filaments seamlessly intersect with intricate, evolving patterns, hinting at a continuous metamorphosis. The luminous light pulses softly, revealing exquisite detail within the transparent material. The background is a soft, out-of-focus bokeh of other glowing intersections within the vast, complex lattice, creating a sense of infinite, ordered complexity and profound depth. The composition is a perfectly symmetrical 1:1 square, centrally focused, with tight macro framing that completely fills the frame with texture and ethereal light. 8k, octane render, raytracing, hyper-detailed, ultra-photorealistic, physically based rendering (PBR), global illumination (GI), cinematic volumetric lighting, sharp focus on subject, subtle depth of field, soft bokeh background.

editorial.headline: Anagram's Joe Eagan: "LPs Make You Dumb," The Case for Permanent Capital
editorial.standfirst: In a defining Breakpoint 2025 keynote, Anagram's co-founder argues the traditional GP/LP model suffocates crypto alpha, proposing a hybrid builder-investor holding company structure instead. This shift from 'allocating' to 'entangling' capital could redefine how institutions deploy into decentralized ecosystems.
key_takeaways: Anagram Staking Services leverages portfolio company Rakurai (Seed led Mar '25) to run the network's highest-performance validator, directly boosting balance sheet yield.
The firm spun out Glider (Oct '25) and its underlying wallet infrastructure Swig, demonstrating how internal 'dogfooding' can birth standalone commercial assets.
Eagan argues the standard '2 and 20' fund model incentivizes asset gathering over returns, whereas Permanent Capital Vehicles (PCVs) align long-term incentives for asymmetric outcomes.
editorial.article_body_markdown:
If the prevailing mood at Breakpoint 2025 is institutional adoption, Joe Eagan came to disrupt the celebration. In a keynote that dismantled the traditional venture capital model, the **Anagram** co-founder argued that the standard General Partner/Limited Partner (GP/LP) structure is fundamentally broken for crypto. His thesis, summarized bluntly as "LPs make you dumb," posits that the rigid mandates, redemption windows, and siloing of financial vs. human capital prevent funds from capturing the true alpha of the blockchain ecosystem. Eagan’s solution is the Permanent Capital Vehicle (PCV)—a holding company structure that allows for an "entangled" approach to building and investing without the pressure of forced selling during volatility.

The power of this model is best illustrated by Anagram's own operational flywheel. Rather than simply buying SOL and paying a third-party validator, the firm launched **Anagram Staking Services** to run its own infrastructure. To maximize yields, they led the seed round for **Rakurai** in March 2025, a high-performance transaction scheduler. By integrating Rakurai’s technology, Anagram now operates the network’s most performant validator. This creates a compounding loop: Anagram’s balance sheet benefits from higher staking yields, Rakurai gains a massive anchor client to prove its tech, and the Solana network gains efficiency. A traditional fund, restricted by mandate to only "invest," would be structurally incapable of executing this technical integration.

This "build and invest" thesis extends beyond infrastructure into consumer applications. Eagan revealed that **Glider**, the automated portfolio management platform launched in October 2025, was born out of Anagram's internal need for better tooling. When the team realized existing wallet infrastructure couldn't support Glider's cross-chain ambitions, they didn't just wait for the market to catch up—they built **Swig**. This programmable smart wallet toolkit now operates as a standalone company, solving a critical gap for the ecosystem while serving as the backbone for Glider. 

For institutional investors, the implication is clear: the era of passive allocation is ending. The outsized returns of the next cycle will not come from picking tokens, but from capital that can actively engineer value. By eliminating the artificial barriers between the balance sheet, the engineering team, and the investment committee, PCVs like Anagram are proving that in crypto, the most effective capital is that which gets its hands dirty.
